title: Cardamom buns
subtitle: Enriched Swedish buns with a cardamom-sugar filling
excerpt: Twelve knot-shaped buns from a lightly enriched yeasted dough filled with cardamom and sugar. The shaping exposes the filling at every layer. Eaten the day they are baked, warm enough that the cardamom-butter filling is still soft.
sourceNotes: The kardemummabulle (cardamom bun) is a documented Scandinavian enriched-bread tradition. Early twentieth-century Swedish domestic cookbooks, many held in public-domain archives, describe enriched yeasted doughs flavoured with cardamom and shaped into knots or spirals. The enriched, egg-and-butter yeasted bun dough is documented across Northern European baking traditions from at least the eighteenth century: Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg, notes cardamom as a spice used in enriched festival breads. The knot shaping technique, which exposes the filling in layers, is consistent across Scandinavian public-domain household manuals of the late nineteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooled buns in a bag. Defrost at room temperature for 30 minutes, then refresh in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: After shaping, arrange on trays, cover and cold-prove overnight. Bake straight from the fridge, adding 2–3 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The enriched dough and sugar filling colour fast — watch after 10 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: The shaping technique used for Swedish cardamom buns: a strip of filled dough is wound around two fingers, then the tail is threaded through the loop and tucked under. The twist pulls the layers apart so the filling is visible at every surface. The shape tightens during baking.

content.text: Cardamom buns are the Scandinavian counterpart to a cinnamon roll, but the shaping is different. Instead of spiralling the dough into a round, you cut strips and tie each one into a knot so the filling shows on every surface. The cardamom filling is intensely aromatic — use freshly ground cardamom if you can, since ground cardamom loses its punch fast.

Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and the {{caster-sugar}} for the dough in a large bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} for the dough and mix with the {{whole-milk}} for the dough. Pour into the flour and mix to a rough dough. Knead for 5 minutes, then add the {{unsalted-butter}} for the dough in four pieces, working each in before adding the next. Knead for a further 5 minutes until the dough is smooth and slightly tacky. It will be softer than a lean dough.
Cover and leave in a warm place for 90 minutes until roughly doubled.
Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} for the filling with the {{caster-sugar}} for the filling and the {{cardamom-green}} until you have a smooth, spreadable paste.

Roll the dough out on a lightly floured surface to a rectangle about 40 × 30 cm. Spread the cardamom filling evenly over the lower half. Fold the bare top half down over the filling to make a 40 × 15 cm rectangle. Press firmly to seal the layers. Cut into 12 strips (each about 3 cm wide). To shape each

: hold one end of the strip and wind the strip loosely around two fingers twice, then push the tail through the loop from below and tuck it under. Place on lined baking trays, leaving 4 cm between each.
Cover loosely and leave for 30 minutes until slightly puffed. Preheat the oven to 200°C fan.

Brush the buns with the beaten {{eggs}} glaze egg, then scatter the {{granulated-sugar}} over the top. Bake for 12–14 minutes until the buns are deep amber and the filling is bubbling at the edges. The base should feel firm when you lift one. Transfer to a rack and eat warm.
